entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: That power belongs to words themselves

entities.name: śakti
entities.cite: That power belongs to words themselves; to the structure of the received language

entities.name: tarka
entities.cite: Tarka as it lives in a reasoner — purusasraya, person-based, the wit of Devadatta

entities.name: puruṣa
entities.cite: the reasoning that rests on a person

entities.name: nyāya
entities.cite: when it follows the words, is interpretive principle

entities.name: āgama
entities.cite: apart from the traditions it is without ground

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: their sakti, their authority, sabdanam eva: belongs to the words themselves

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: Tarka as it lives in a reasoner — purusasraya, person-based

axioms.name: sabdanugata
axioms.cite: is authoritative only sabda-anugata, when it follows the words: tracking, subserving, drawing out

axioms.name: anibandhana
axioms.cite: Cut loose — anagamesu, where there is no received text to follow — it is anibandhana, groundless

denials.name: svatantra
denials.cite: reasoning is real as an *instrument of the word* and nothing as an independent source

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: That power belongs to words themselves

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: the reasoning that rests on a person

rejected_readings.label: tarka_coequal
rejected_readings.rendering: The personal reasoning power and word-power are essentially equivalent sources of interpretive authority; both work together as equal instruments.
rejected_readings.why: This misses the verse's radical subordination: sakti belongs to words; tarka has authority only when following words. The commentary emphasizes asymmetry, not equivalence. Reading tarka and sakti as coequal violates the axiomatic structure.

rejected_readings.label: tarka_independent
rejected_readings.rendering: Reasoning, when exercised by a skilled person, can be an independent and authoritative source of truth, even without textual support.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly denies this: tarka without agama is anibandhana (groundless). The verse rules out freestanding authority for reasoning.

notes: The verse settles a key epistemological question: reasoning (tarka) is not rejected, but its authority is strictly conditional — it works only as an instrument of word-power (sabda-sakti) and tradition (agama). The Vakyapadiya's own arguments claim authority not through independent wit but through fidelity to the grammarians' teachings, exemplifying the doctrine it states.
